gist: In a mid-year webinar, Meb Faber argues US stocks are historically expensive (CAPE ~42, dividend yield ~1%) and destined for low-single-digit returns over 5-7 years, while foreign, emerging, small-cap value, real assets and T-bills offer far better risk/reward. He details Cambria's shareholder-yield, endowment-replication, trend and hedging funds and flags two non-consensus positions: allocating up to 50% to trend, and holding only T-bills in fixed income until spreads compensate for risk.

insights.claim: US equities are trading at a Shiller CAPE ratio of roughly 42 with a dividend yield of about 1%, which Meb Faber flags as unambiguously expensive across every valuation metric (CAPE, forward PE, trailing PE, price/cash flow, dividend yield).
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A starting valuation this high is a structural headwind for US index returns; investors should temper S&P 500 return expectations and look for cheaper exposures.
insights.quote: We're at 42 and change. You know US dividend yield is 1%. So there's no question US stocks are expensive.

insights.claim: Faber says no market that has ended a year at a CAPE ratio of 40 has ever produced above-average returns over the following decade, giving expensive markets a historical batting average of zero for beating the average.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Frames elevated CAPE as gravity/weight on the next 10 years of returns rather than a crash signal, arguing for lower US-equity allocation now.
insights.quote: Not once have we found in history that the a market that ends the year at a cape ratio of 40 has above average returns the next decade. So the batting average historically has been zero

insights.claim: Faber warns there is no ceiling on valuation, citing that following Japan's historical path would mean a double from current US levels and that South Korea's market roughly tripled in the past year.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Cautions against shorting or fully exiting expensive markets on valuation alone, since overvaluation can extend dramatically before mean-reverting.
insights.quote: there's no ceiling on valuation. And so, you know, we we look at the example of Japan and say, "Hey, you know, this could if if we followed the track of Japan, this that's a double from here."

insights.claim: Faber argues sentiment is not yet euphoric despite high valuations, pointing to University of Michigan consumer sentiment near all-time lows and AAII and Investors Intelligence readings that are not euphoric, meaning the final euphoric blow-off top has not occurred.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Suggests the bull market could still have a euphoric top left, so bearish valuation calls need not be imminent; watch sentiment for the blow-off signal.
insights.quote: the sentiment is universally not euphoric. Michigan sentiment is like all-time lows. AI not that euphoric. Investors intelligence not that euphoric.

insights.claim: Faber expects the US stock market to perform poorly over the next five to seven years, pegging returns at low single digits and firmly below 15%.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A concrete low-return forecast that argues for diversifying away from US-cap-weighted equities into cheaper asset classes.
insights.quote: How do I think the stock market will do in the next five to seven years? Poorly. Um lower than 15% for sure. Expectations, you know, I would peg it low single digits.

themes.theme: Rotation into foreign, EM and value

insights.claim: Faber contends the value rotation into foreign and emerging markets is not over, since many foreign markets still trade at low-teens CAPE valuations versus roughly 42 for the US, even after Taiwan and South Korea have surged.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Argues investors who feel they missed the run still have a large valuation gap to exploit by adding foreign and EM exposure.
insights.quote: many of these foreign markets are still teens, low teens valuations relative to the US. I'm talking cape ratio at 42

insights.claim: Faber recommends a default global equity allocation of 60% US, 30% foreign developed and 10% emerging markets as a starting point, versus the roughly two-thirds US Bogle index weighting.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A concrete allocation blueprint; simply moving to index weights would materially cut most investors' US overexposure.
insights.quote: 60% US, 30% foreign developed, 10% emerging. That's the starting point.

insights.claim: Faber identifies the two most common flaws in traditional portfolios as being far too concentrated in highly-valued US large-cap stocks and holding very little foreign, emerging-market or real-asset exposure (commodities, commodity equities, TIPS and REITs).
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A checklist for repositioning: break the US market-cap tilt and add real assets to improve expected 1-5 year outcomes.
insights.quote: the two biggest, you know, kind of, uh, problems when we see traditional portfolios, one is they're way too US focused... And then the second is very few people own real assets.

insights.claim: Faber notes US small-cap value is having a monster year in 2026, an outperformance almost no one anticipated, signaling that value is rotating within the US market and not only abroad.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Points to a broadening rotation away from mega-cap growth; small-cap value may continue to lead as the cap-weight tilt unwinds.
insights.quote: Small cap having a monster year uh uh which no one said that in forever, right?

insights.claim: Faber predicts explosive flows into long-neglected asset classes like emerging and foreign markets as advisors shift from having sold them to fearing they will miss another 20-30% gain, reframing the theme as moving from a bear market in diversification to a bull market in diversification.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Anticipates a FOMO-driven flow surge into cheap non-US assets, especially once the S&P stalls; early positioning captures the re-rating.
insights.quote: then you might see some pretty explosive flows into these asset classs that have been long uh you know long forgotten about put in the dumpster bin of allocations.

insights.claim: Faber argues equal-weighted indexing will beat cap-weighted indexing over the next decade because the Achilles heel of cap weighting is valuation when markets get extreme, as they are now.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Favors equal-weight over cap-weight S&P exposure to avoid concentration in the most overvalued mega-caps.
insights.quote: Equal weighted over cap weighted. cap weighted the the the Achilles heel of cap weighting is valuation when things go crazy like right now uh I think evil weight is going to stomp cap weight for the next decade

insights.claim: Faber invokes Jack Bogle's 1999-2000 shift from 75% to 25% stocks as a template for today, noting the same conditions now hold: expensive stocks, bonds yielding 4%, and attractively valued foreign, emerging and small-value equities.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Uses the 'world's greatest market timer' framing to justify trimming US equity risk when competing assets finally offer real yield and value.
insights.quote: Jack's out here saying, "Hey, I moved my allocation from 75% stocks to 25% stocks." And to me, we call him the world's greatest market timer.

insights.claim: Cambria's shareholder-yield ETFs SYLD (US), FYLD (foreign) and EYLD (emerging) now have 10-year track records, share the same methodology and similar valuation and cash-flow characteristics, yet produce very different sector exposures.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Shows that identical value screens abroad yield different sector bets, so owning all three diversifies factor exposure rather than duplicating it.
insights.quote: if you compare all these funds, they have similar characteristics, but totally different sector exposures.

insights.claim: Faber notes the foreign-developed shareholder-yield fund is maximally long energy at roughly a 30% position while the emerging-market fund carries a huge tech weighting and the US fund has almost no tech, upending the assumption that value investing avoids technology.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Investors can get tech and energy exposure through value screens abroad; sector outcomes depend on geography, not the value label.
insights.quote: the foreign developed max long energy. I mean this is like a 30% position the uh emerging fund huge tech position whereas in the US it has almost no tech.

insights.claim: Faber says Cambria avoids value traps by running a final relative-strength/momentum sort on the shortlist of shareholder-yield candidates rather than on both entry and exit, a process that historically added about 1% per year in simulated returns.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A concrete, low-turnover way to screen out cheap-and-falling stocks; the ~1% annual edge is a repeatable factor overlay.
insights.quote: using relative strength adds about a percent a year in you know hypothetical returns

insights.claim: Faber reveals the biggest winners inside Cambria's FYLD, EYLD and GVAL value funds have been AI and semiconductor-adjacent companies that doubled, tripled or quadrupled, forcing the funds to continually trim so no single stock exceeds 10-20% of the portfolio.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Even value funds captured the AI/semi run via cheap foreign names; the forced trimming is a built-in profit-taking discipline.
insights.quote: the big investments we've had in those funds that have screamed higher have been AI semiconductor adjacent companies.

insights.claim: Faber's answer for fixed-income yield is T-bills, arguing that investing further out the curve only pays when yields offer a margin of safety above the risk-free rate, and that Cambria's TYLD fund remains 100% in T-bills because no bond sleeve currently offers a wide enough spread.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Argues against reaching for duration or credit now; hold T-bills until spreads compensate for risk.
insights.quote: where should I look for yield on the fixed income side and my answer to that is T- billills.

insights.claim: Faber warns corporate and high-yield credit spreads are in the top decile or top 1% of tightness historically, meaning small increases in volatility could blow spreads out by percentage points, so portfolios stuffed with agg, corporates or high yield are poorly compensated.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Flags credit as an asymmetric risk; underweight IG/HY and hold T-bills to avoid spread widening losses.
insights.quote: corporates in high yield are like top decile or top 1% of spread historically and if we know anything about that... those spreads blow out percentage points

insights.claim: Faber attributes today's tight credit spreads to price-insensitive institutional buyers who, after years of near-zero yields, are eager to buy corporate bonds simply because they now yield around 5%, regardless of the margin of safety.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Explains why spreads stay tight and why the setup is fragile; a supply of yield-chasing buyers can reverse quickly.
insights.quote: I think it's a lot of price insensitive buyers.

insights.claim: Faber holds the non-consensus view that up to 50% of a portfolio can be allocated to trend following, versus the 10-20% that even trend believers typically use, valuing it as a diversifier that performs when other assets fail.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A far larger trend allocation than industry norm; frames managed futures as a strategic, not fringe, portfolio pillar.
insights.quote: One is how much of a role trend plays in the portfolio. Longtime followers will know that my comment is 50%. You could argue up to 50. No one on the planet does that.

insights.claim: Faber highlights that managed futures is one of the few strategies that can short bonds and is currently short bonds, having been a key diversifier in 2022 when both stocks and bonds were wrecked.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Managed futures offers negative correlation to bonds in a rising-rate/inflation regime; its current short-bond stance is a hedge worth watching.
insights.quote: It's one of the few things that can be short bonds. So when everything got wrecked in 2022, uh it was one of the few uh diversifiers that was short bonds. It's currently short bonds today.

insights.claim: Faber describes Cambria's Trinity (TRTY) strategy as half a globally diversified buy-and-hold of stocks, bonds and real assets (none market-cap weighted) and half momentum and trend, and says it holds most of his own public money.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A skin-in-the-game blueprint blending non-cap-weighted global assets with trend for both statistical and psychological diversification.
insights.quote: the trinity concept is a half of the allocation is a global buy and hold of the world asset classes... And then the other half of Trinity is momentum and trend.

insights.claim: Faber argues cap-weighted market indexing like the S&P 500 is itself the world's ultimate trend-following algorithm, since holdings are sized by price times shares outstanding, so investors own more as stocks rise and less as they fall.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Reframes passive indexing as an unmanaged trend strategy, undercutting objections to explicit trend following on narrative grounds.
insights.quote: S&P and market cap indexing is the world's ultimate trend following index... you own mo more. The... algorithm sizing is you own more as it goes up and you own less as it goes down.

insights.claim: Cambria's Endowment ETF (ENDW) replicates Yale/Harvard-style endowment allocations using a global factor-based portfolio with about 40% leverage (140% gross exposure) at an all-in cost near 25 basis points, and now has a one-year-plus track record Faber intends to publicly benchmark against Calpers, Harvard and Bridgewater.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Offers a sub-30bp liquid, tax-efficient alternative to expensive endowment/private-equity complexity; the public scoreboard is a catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: we think you can replicate the majority of what these great endowments do with a global factor-based allocation but also with a little leverage. So ENDW can is like 40% leverage.

insights.claim: Faber argues the low-cost endowment-replication approach gets little coverage because an entire ecosystem of consultants, private-equity advisors and newsletters profits from institutional complexity and is not incentivized to acknowledge a cheap ETF can match it.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Structural conflict of interest keeps fees high in institutional allocation; a disruptor product could gain share as results are published.
insights.quote: there's an entire ecosystem... hundreds of thousands of positions, private equity consultants, family office, email newsletters, endowment magazines. There's a whole world ecosystem that profits on this complexity.

insights.claim: Faber describes Cambria's VAMO ETF as a fund that buys US value-and-trend stocks and dynamically shorts broad S&P futures from 0 to 100% (half by valuation, half by trend), currently about half hedged given expensive-but-rising markets, and removes hedges after a market decline.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A tool for reluctant bulls to stay invested with a variable hedge that lifts after crashes rather than staying market-neutral into a recovery.
insights.quote: Vammo, which is buys a portfolio of of US stocks with good value and trend and then it shorts broad-based S&P futures from anywhere from 0 to 100%... So, that fund is half hedged.

insights.claim: Faber describes Cambria's TAIL fund as an insurance-like vehicle buying puts on the US stock market coupled with 10-year Treasuries, which is expected to lose money over time but can protect a portfolio during a tail event.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A defined-cost hedge that investors typically add tactically as markets start to roll over; useful for tail-risk protection.
insights.quote: Tail is a inverse style fund. It's buying puts on the US stock market coupled with 10-year bonds... it's probably going to lose money like an insurance vehicle, but some years... it it can save your hide

insights.claim: Faber says Cambria's global REIT fund is up about 12% year-to-date, a surprise strong year for a value-tilted global real-estate strategy that he had not expected.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Global value-tilted REITs are quietly outperforming; real-estate exposure is delivering within the under-owned real-assets bucket.
insights.quote: would he expect REITs global REITs to be up 12%. I would have said no. Uh but here we are. REITs are having a really phenomenal year

insights.claim: Faber says gold plays both a strategic and tactical role in a portfolio and that it is hard to argue against gold improving risk-adjusted investor returns over time.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Supports a persistent gold allocation as a risk-adjusted return enhancer alongside other real assets.
insights.quote: gold. I think yeah, it it definitely plays a role strategic as well as tactical in a portfolio. Uh it's hard to argue against that uh gold improves the risk investor returns over time.

insights.claim: Faber predicts a revolution in taxable-account asset location, with investors splitting between growth-oriented holders who want to avoid dividends and their taxes and income-oriented holders who want them; Cambria's TAX ETF targets low-to-no-yield US stocks to serve the former.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Signals a growing product category around tax-aware equity exposure; low-dividend ETFs become more valuable if the S&P dividend yield rises from all-time lows.
insights.quote: I think you're going to see a revolution in taxable accounts on asset location... We launched TAX, which is an ETF in the US that avoids high dividend companies

insights.claim: Faber says Cambria has completed five 351 exchange conversions totaling nearly a billion dollars in assets, with another (a global stock fund) coming in the fall, and offers custom 351s for advisors or institutions with large concentrated-stock positions.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: 351 conversions are a growing tax-deferral tool for concentrated-stock holders; a live channel for advisors seeking custom ETF wrappers.
insights.quote: 351s which we haven't even talked about today. We've now done five of these almost a billion in assets.

insights.claim: Faber calls a low-cost farmland interval fund a billion-dollar idea, noting farmland has historically been hard to access with only two or three REITs and platforms like Farmland Partners and AcreTrader offering fractionalized ownership; he recently sold his own farmland because it became too large a position.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Highlights an underserved real-asset niche and a potential product/investment opportunity in accessible farmland vehicles.
insights.quote: if I was a younger not doing what I was doing now, I would certainly start an interval fund based on farmland. I think that's a billion dollar idea.

insights.claim: Faber recommends holding Bitcoin only as its market-portfolio weight, currently around half a percent, and notes investors already gain crypto exposure through broad equity indices as crypto-adjacent companies rise within them.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A market-cap-weighted, low-conviction crypto stance; investors get incidental crypto exposure via equity indices without overweighting.
insights.quote: own it as a percentage of the market portfolio. Uh, which today what half percent or something.

insights.claim: Faber warns investors underestimate how large the biggest private US companies have become, arguing the 'big three' if they come to market would be larger than the entire energy sector, which could trigger a significant IPO cycle and equity supply.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A wave of mega-IPOs would add substantial equity supply and could weigh on valuations; watch for the IPO cycle as a supply signal.
insights.quote: the the big three if they come to market will be larger than the entire energy sector, which is astonishing to me

insights.claim: Faber warns that public displays of hubris tend to precede sharp reversals, citing Kathy Wood mocking value and buy-and-hold investors near the peak of ARK and pointing to Michael Saylor's recent Bitcoin-yield and AI video claims as a similar red flag.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Treats promoter hubris as a sentiment top-signal; extreme bragging around ARK-style and Bitcoin narratives is a caution flag.
insights.quote: You guys remember when Kathy Wood was making fun of value investors and buy and hold investors right before like it was like the peak of Arc... the market gods do not reward that.

insights.claim: Faber argues the belief that US stocks are expensive is now a consensus view, with nearly every advisor holding heavily-valued S&P market-cap positions while confident they can exit before the market rolls over, echoing the 'wonderful ball' metaphor where none of the clocks have hands.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Warns that the crowded 'I'll get out in time' trade rarely works; the crowded exit is itself the risk when the market turns.
insights.quote: almost every single one thinks that they can make a move before it rolls over... none of the clocks have hands.

insights.claim: Faber frames buying the dip in US equities as a 250-year phenomenon, using the long history of American equity ownership to argue that even severe drawdowns like the Great Depression and Global Financial Crisis barely register on a multi-century chart and are followed by strong long-run returns.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A long-horizon behavioral anchor for staying invested and buying downturns, even while near-term US return expectations are low.
insights.quote: buy the dip has been going on for 250 years.
